questions.text: I do not naturally read the social meaning behind gift-giving or gesture exchanges.
I batch similar tasks together to work more efficiently.
I tend to process experiences evenly rather than in dramatic peaks and valleys.
I hold myself accountable when I fail to respond to suffering that I was aware of.
I find that others naturally look to me for direction.
I hold the view that healthy self-interest is the bedrock of a productive life.
I find that my body reacts strongly to emotional situations — racing heart, flushed skin, tight chest.
I experience a new environment as a barrage of stimulation that takes time to process.
I find that emotional distance from the details of a case allows me to make recommendations that are more consistent and less biased.
I hold the view that discipline is the bridge between goals and accomplishment.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the consideration that should humble every other claim on my attention and resources.
I am not afraid to overrule others when the stakes are high.
I feel that the group should find its own way rather than rely on one person.
I believe that a good day is one where I gave more than I received.
I notice when a seemingly spontaneous remark was actually calculated.
I can usually tell when someone has been crying even if they have composed themselves.
I feel comfortable dropping a project when my interest shifts to something more compelling.
I routinely deplete my own reserves to make sure others are taken care of.
